items.text: Margin of safety is just the gap between what a business is worth and what you pay. Worth ₹58,000cr, trading at ₹43,500cr? You're buying at a 25% discount — and that gap is your cushion if you've judged the future slightly wrong. The formula: (value − price) ÷ value. If you can't state the number, you don't have a margin of safety — just hope.

A stock down 45% from its high is NOT automatically 'safe'. That high might have been madness to begin with — fall 45% from a crazy price and you can still be overpaying. Safety is measured against what the business is worth, never against where the price used to be. A stock can drop 80% and still be dear; another can sit at an all-time high and be a bargain.

A big margin of safety means you don't need to be precise. If a business is worth somewhere around ₹60–80 and you can buy at ₹30, it doesn't matter whether the real answer is ₹62 or ₹79 — you're miles below either. Precision only matters when you're paying close to full value. That's why the classic move is to hunt for the obviously cheap, not the exactly-measured.
A colleague: 'This stock is down 45% from its high — that's automatic margin of safety.' Best response?
options: Agree — big drops mean big safety
The high may have been mispriced; MoS is price vs intrinsic value, not vs history
Wait for a 60% drop to be safe
items.explanation: Margin of safety is anchored to your estimate of intrinsic value. Price history — highs, lows, averages — is not evidence of value.

items.text: You estimate intrinsic value at ₹800/share; the stock trades at ₹600. What is the margin of safety?
options: 25%, the discount of price to value
items.explanation: MoS = (IV − P) / IV = (800−600)/800 = 25%. The upside-to-price (33%) is a different number — don't confuse the two denominators.

items.text: A quality firm's margins compress from 21% to 16% on a one-off input-cost spike; the stock falls 35%. Its headline P/E barely drops. Where might the hidden margin of safety be?
options: There is none — the P/E proves it's still expensive
In normalised earnings: at recovered margins the true P/E is far lower
In the falling share price alone
items.explanation: Reported earnings are depressed, inflating the P/E. Recompute on normalised margins: if earnings recover ~30%, the real multiple is far cheaper than the headline.

items.text: Open Asian Paints on screener.in. Find today's P/E (price ÷ per-share profit) and its 5-year median P/E, both shown on the page. Is the stock cheaper or dearer than its own history? Important catch: a below-average P/E is only a hint, not a margin of safety — that still depends on what the business is truly worth. Write one line: cheaper or dearer than usual, and does that alone justify buying? (~5 min)
